title: Chapter 39: The Capture
content: They surfaced at dawn because Ash said the shift change at the perimeter stations created a ninety-second gap in drone coverage. Ninety seconds to get from the tunnel exit to the supply cache that Silas had mapped six weeks ago — a maintenance closet beneath a parking structure, stocked with water tabs and protein bars and medical supplies that would keep them alive for another week.
Ninety seconds. She could glitch her way through ninety seconds.

The manhole cover scraped aside. Gray light poured down — the flat metallic light of a city that never quite woke up, just cycled through different shades of surveillance. Wren climbed out first. The air hit her like a wall: exhaust fumes and wet concrete and the sour smell of trash trucks beginning their routes. A delivery drone buzzed overhead and she glitched it — a quick pulse, just enough to make its camera record static for three seconds.

Behind her, Ash emerged. He moved wrong — she could see it in the set of his shoulders, the careful way he held his left arm against his body. The wound was worse than he'd admitted. But he moved fast, and they stuck to the wall of the parking structure, and the ninety seconds started counting down in her head.
Seventy seconds.

They reached the maintenance door. Ash punched the code — 7-4-7-1 — and the lock clicked and they slipped inside. The closet was narrow, chemical-lit, shelves loaded with supplies that made Wren's mouth water just looking at them. She grabbed a water bottle and drank half of it in three seconds.
Then she heard the sound.

Not servos. Not drones. Something lower — a vibration she felt through the floor, through the shelf she was leaning against, through her own bones. A rhythmic thudding, like metal footsteps on concrete, and it was getting closer.
Ash heard it too. He went still — that absolute stillness that meant every nerve in his body was firing, every instinct screaming *run*.
"Ash—"
"Quiet."
The thudding stopped.
Then the wall exploded inward.

Not the whole wall — a section of cinderblock three feet wide, blown apart by a breaching charge that Wren didn't even hear until after the dust was in her lungs and her ears were screaming with the aftershock. Light poured through the gap. Figures moved behind it. And then the Hounds came through.
Six of them.

The machines poured into the closet like water through a broken dam. Quadruped frames, low-slung, sensor arrays rotating on their backs like the spines of mechanical predators. Their legs ended in grip pads that found purchase on any surface. Their chassis were matte black — no reflection, no signature, nothing to see until they were already on top of you.
And behind them: the containment team. Four people in tactical gear, tranq rifles raised, moving with the coordinated precision of people who had done this many times before.
The Hounds had let them surface. Tracked Ash's heat signature through the tunnels, followed at a distance, waited until there was no crowd, no cover, nowhere to run.
A trap. The whole thing was a trap.

Ash moved before Wren could think. He grabbed the nearest shelf and *pulled* — ripped it off the wall with a sound like tearing metal — and swung it into the first Hound. The machine went down, legs scrabbling, sensor array cracked. Ash was already on the second one. He jumped on its back and seized the sensor array with both hands and *tore* it free. Sparks erupted. The Hound spasmed and went still.

Two more came at him from opposite sides. He kicked one in the sensor housing and the metal crumpled. The other one got close — too close — and its grip pad locked around his ankle and *yanked*. He went down hard on the concrete floor and the breath left his lungs in a grunt.
"Ash!"
Wren reached for her glitch. The cameras — she could fuzz the cameras, blind the containment team, buy them seconds—

A Hound turned toward her. Its sensor array rotated and locked and she felt the infrared sweep across her body like a searchlight and she pushed and the world went white and the cameras in the parking structure shrieked and died.
Three seconds of blindness for every surveillance device within range. Three seconds where no one could see anything.

Ash was on his feet again. He'd gotten clear of the grip pad and now he had a piece of rebar from the shattered wall and he was swinging it like a man who had nothing left to lose. The rebar caught a Hound in the midsection and the machine folded. He brought it down on another one's sensor array and the glass shattered and the Hound veered sideways and crashed into the wall.

But there were too many. Even broken, even damaged, the machines kept coming. The containment team advanced behind them — methodical, patient, letting the Hounds do the close work while they lined up their shots.
Wren saw the rifle rise. She saw the scope find Ash's center mass. She screamed his name and he turned—
The dart hit his neck.

A small sound. A hiss of compressed air and a wet *thck* of the needle finding flesh. Ash's hand went to his throat. His fingers closed around the dart and he *pulled* but the plunger was already depressing and whatever was in that syringe was already flooding his bloodstream.
He staggered. The rebar dropped from his grip and clattered on the floor.
Two more darts. One in the shoulder. One in the thigh. Overkill. They wanted him down. They wanted him *certainly* down.
"Ash — ASH—"

He fell to his knees. His tattoos were blazing — all of them, every line, every geometric spiral, burning blue-white like circuits overloaded. The light pulsed erratically, stuttering, like a heart in fibrillation. He looked at her.

The containment team was moving fast now. Two of them had Ash — grabbing him under the arms, dragging him toward the breach in the wall. The Hounds were forming a perimeter, sensor arrays rotating, and one of them turned toward Wren and she felt the targeting lock like a finger on her spine.
She had seconds.
She could go after him. Could push past the Hounds, fight through the team, reach him before they got him through the wall. She could—

She would be captured too. Two subjects for the price of one. Everything they had — the Signal Key, the files, the plan — all of it gone. Hannah Marsh and Devi Chandra and Marcus Fell and every other name on that drive, their stories buried forever because Wren couldn't let go.
Or she could run.
The Hound's targeting lock tightened. The containment team was already at the breach. Ash's head lolled, his body going slack, but his gaze found her one more time through half-open lids.
"Run," he said. The word was barely a sound. Just breath. Just a syllable pushed past lips that were already going numb. "Publish the files."
His eyes found hers. Held.
"Don't come for me."
Then the containment team pulled him through the wall and he was gone and Wren was standing in a ruined closet with a Hound's targeting lock on her chest and a choice that wasn't a choice at all.
She ran.

Back through the maintenance door. Down the stairwell. Into the basement level where the parking structure's server hub hummed behind a locked door she glitched open without breaking stride. Through the server room — the noise of the machines swallowing her footsteps, her heartbeat, her ragged breathing. Out the far side into a service tunnel that led — somewhere, anywhere, away.
She ran until her legs gave out. Then she crawled behind a junction box and pressed herself into the corner and shook.
Ash was gone.
The Hounds had him. NeuroScale had him. Subject 11 — recaptured. Returned to containment.
And she —
She was alone.

The same aloneness she'd felt before the Underlayer, before the community, before any of it. That vast hollow space where another person should have been. The silence that wasn't silence but *absence*, the empty room where someone's voice used to live.
No. Not the same. Not anymore.
She had the Signal Key in her pack. The drive with the files — twelve names plus hers. She had a glitch that was evolving into something she didn't fully understand. She had a promise.
*Publish the files. Don't come for me.*
Two instructions. One she could follow. The other —
She pressed her forehead against her knees. Her shoulders heaved. No tears — her body was too depleted, too empty, too gutted for tears. Just the shaking. Just the hollow.
Then she stopped.

She stopped shaking. She stopped hiding. She pulled the drive out of her pack and held it in both hands and stared at the small rectangle of plastic and metal that contained the evidence of thirteen ruined lives.
She'd said she'd publish them. She'd said she'd make the world see.
She'd said she'd always find him.

The junction box hummed. The server room's machines breathed their electronic breath. And somewhere in the city above, the Hounds were carrying Ash Corrigan back to the place he'd escaped three years ago.
Wren stood up.
She was alone. But not the same person who had walked into the Underlayer. Not the same person who had hidden in apartment blocks and prayed the cameras wouldn't find her. Not the same person at all.
She had the Signal Key. She had the files. She had powers still growing — Stage 4, unprecedented, the same classification that had made Ash a priority target.
And she had a promise to keep.
The war wasn't over.
It was just getting started.
